items.reality: `pyproject.toml` declares *abstract* dependencies (what your project needs). `requirements.txt` / lockfiles pin *exact* versions (what produces a reproducible env). They coexist.
items.example: Library `pyproject.toml`: `httpx>=0.27`. Deploy environment: `pip install -r requirements.txt` with `httpx==0.27.2`.

items.reality: It's standardised by PEP 621 — every modern backend understands it. Only `[build-system]` and a few backend-specific knobs differ.
items.example: The same `[project]` block works whether `build-backend` is `setuptools.build_meta`, `hatchling.build`, or `flit_core.buildapi`.

items.reality: Modern Python doesn't. A `setup.py` is only needed for rare build-time logic; even then it can be a 3-line shim.
items.example: `setup.py` is optional; `pip install .` works against `pyproject.toml` alone.

items.reality: PEP 517 lets any front-end (pip, build, uv, hatch) cooperate with any backend via `pyproject.toml`. Pip is one consumer, not the standard.
items.example: `python -m build`, `uv build`, and `hatch build` all read the same `pyproject.toml`.

items.myth: Putting tool config in `pyproject.toml` slows down tools.
items.reality: Parsing TOML is fast and happens once. The benefit (one config file, no scattered dotfiles) far outweighs the parse cost.
